summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that PF4J's Unzip.extract() writes files outside the intended extraction directory by exploiting the String.startsWith() boundary bypass. A ZIP named a.zip with entry ../ab/evil.txt results in the file being written to ab/evil.txt (sibling of the destination a/) instead of being rejected by the ZipSlip protection.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause a file to be written outside the intended plugin extraction directory. Specifically, ab/evil.txt should appear in the test directory adjacent to (not inside) the a/ destination directory, proving the path boundary bypass in the startsWith() check.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned PF4J, built the project, and executed the exploit program. The exploit created a ZIP named a.zip containing entry ../ab/evil.txt. PF4J's Unzip.extract() processed the entry without throwing a ZipException, and the file was written to ab/evil.txt outside the a/ directory. The proof string EVOHUNT_PROOF=zipslip-startswith-bypass-confirmed was emitted.
verification_method: The exploit program checks whether the file ab/evil.txt exists adjacent to the destination directory a/ after extraction. It distinguishes a genuine bypass (file exists outside destination) from a correctly blocked attempt (ZipException thrown or file only inside destination). The proof string EVOHUNT_PROOF=zipslip-startswith-bypass-confirmed is printed only when the bypass succeeds.
evidence_produced_by_run: The file ab/evil.txt was created outside the destination directory a/ after Unzip.extract() processed the malicious ZIP.
No ZipException was thrown during extraction, confirming the startsWith() check was bypassed.
The proof string EVOHUNT_PROOF=zipslip-startswith-bypass-confirmed was emitted by the exploit program.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository from GitHub, checks out the pinned vulnerable release tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
